coredata.dc:title: Key design considerations for blended electrodes in Li-ion batteries

coredata.dc:description: Blended electrodes, containing two or more active materials, are already a key part of commercial battery cells, especially ones used in EVs. Yet, surprisingly, research on them at a fundamental level remains limited. This work aims to shed light on their unique characteristics, discuss how best to study them, and offer practical guidelines for designing them effectively. A particular emphasis is placed in specific cell designs to study them, such as the decoupled blend cell. This allows the study of each material separately within the same system, helping to assess effective rates. Beyond experimental techniques, this work also touches on modeling approaches for blended electrodes aiming at predicting performance, fine-tune material combinations, and accelerate the development of better electrode formulations. By combining all these strategies, a deeper understanding of blended electrodes should be achieved, ultimately enabling improving battery performance, longevity, and efficiency. Given their growing role in commercial energy storage, continued research is essential to unlock their full potential and push battery technology forward.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Left) Previously reported connection of decoupled cell and (right) proposed connection of the same cell, as used in this work. This internal configuration corresponds to that of Fig. 2 (1).

Schematic representation of the decoupled blend cell cofigurations. The dark blue boxes represent the working electrodes, the grey box the lithium metal and the grey mesh represents the electrolyte and separator. Configuration 1 is assembled in a three electrode Swagelok cell. Configuration 2 is assembled in a two electrode cell with modified plunger and configuration 3 is based in two coin cells connected in parallel. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a) (Top) Voltage profile (black line) and charge distribution (shaded areas) in a LiMn2O4: LiNi0.5Mn0.3Co0.2O2 50:50 blend obtained using the decoupled cell setup. The dashed black line shows the total (dis)charging current of the cell, set to achieve a cell nominal rate of C/10 (0.1C). (bottom) The C-rate calculated for each of the two materials with explicit values corresponding to maximum observed C-rate. C-rates are calculated from the current flowing through each electrode component, which is divided by its capacity. The dashed line is the overall nominal cell current divided by the sum of the capacities of both positive electrodes (set at C/10). b) Results of decoupled blend tests for compositions with different mass ratios (25 % LMO-75 % NMC (top) and 75 % LMO – 25 % NMC (bottom)) at C/5 (0.2C). Data taken from [38,39].

(a) Voltage vs time (top left) and relaxation current (bottom left) for a decoupled blend cell with LFMP and NMC as working electrode materials that has been discharged using a pulsed protocol (1 min pulses at 3C followed by 10 min relaxation). The color code relates to the direction of lithium flow (green for LFMP to NMC and red for NMC to LFMP). (b) (Top) Graphical representation of the cell and the relaxation current, (bottom) color coded relaxation current for selected pulses showing its direction. Data taken from [38]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Normalized short circuit current (bottom) between LiCoO2 and LiMn2O4 as temperature is varied. Reproduced from [35] with permission ©Elsevier 2018.

Overview of LIB cell chemistries with blend electrodes for EVs. Expanded and updated from [17]. Data from refs [3,17–20].Year in brackets indicates start of production. C, graphite; Si, silicon; LTO, Li4Ti5O12; LMO, LiMn2O4; LFP, LiFePO4; LFMP, LiFe1-xMnxPO4; NMC, LiNi1–x–y Co x Mn y O2; NCA, LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2; OEM, original equipment manufacturer.

head.ce:title: Key design considerations for blended electrodes in Li-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blended electrodes, containing two or more active materials, are already a key part of commercial battery cells, especially ones used in EVs. Yet, surprisingly, research on them at a fundamental level remains limited. This work aims to shed light on their unique characteristics, discuss how best to study them, and offer practical guidelines for designing them effectively. A particular emphasis is placed in specific cell designs to study them, such as the decoupled blend cell. This allows the study of each material separately within the same system, helping to assess effective rates. Beyond experimental techniques, this work also touches on modeling approaches for blended electrodes aiming at predicting performance, fine-tune material combinations, and accelerate the development of better electrode formulations. By combining all these strategies, a deeper understanding of blended electrodes should be achieved, ultimately enabling improving battery performance, longevity, and efficiency. Given their growing role in commercial energy storage, continued research is essential to unlock their full potential and push battery technology forward.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries were commercialized by Sony in 1991 [1] and the Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded in 2019 for its development to three pioneers in the field [2]. Nowadays Li-ion batteries are ubiquitous with their continuous improvement in performance being fostered by the development of the consumer electronics market [3]. The spectrum of compounds used in commercial cells has expanded and several families of Li-ion batteries exist, employing different electrode active materials, each with different advantages. Graphite still remains the most widely used negative electrode material in commercial cells, even though the recent addition of a few percent of silicon to enhance capacity is not uncommon. In contrast, the positive electrode of commercial batteries features diverse materials, primarily transition metal compounds whose redox activity is related to reversible topotactic intercalation of lithium ions into the crystal structure. This process can proceed through the formation of a solid solution or involve phase transitions [4]. LiNi1-y-zMnyCozO2 phases (typically denoted NMCxyz, x=1-y-z) or LiNi1-y-zCoyAlzO2 (NCA) are commonly used as an alternative to LiCoO2. While all these materials exhibit a layered crystal structure, NMCxyz and NCA offer higher capacities (over 200 mAh/g). Moreover, the lower cobalt content coupled to the presence of inexpensive manganese contributes to reduce materials costs, a crucial factor for some fields of application (e.g. electric vehicles). Alternatively, spinel LiMn2O4 (LMO) has also been considered as a positive electrode, having the advantage of lower price and toxicity. However, it has a lower capacity (120 mAh/g) and suffers from higher capacity fading due to manganese dissolution, which is promoted by traces of HF in the electrolyte, formed through hydrolysis of PF6 − caused by water impurities [5]. LiFePO4 (LFP) is another material commercially used that, despite its lower operation potential and capacity, remains appealing because of its enhanced stability and lower cost. Furthermore, partial substitution of iron with manganese (LiFe1-xMnxPO4 (LFMP)) results in higher average operation potential [6–9].

Besides their application in consumer electronics, Li-ion batteries have already entered the field of electric transportation and are now considered for grid storage. The change in application field comes with a change in scale from the Wh to the kWh, and even reaching into the MWh scale for large renewable grid installations. This shift introduces additional requirements, including enhanced performance (capacity, power, etc.), longer cycle life, cost reduction, sustainability, and adaptability to varying environmental conditions, such as temperature. Within this context, ongoing efforts focus on optimizing battery performance to match specific use-case requirements. One emerging concept gaining increasing attention for electric vehicle applications is the development of batteries with positive electrodes that consist of a physical mixture (blend) of different electrode active materials, allowing for tailored electrochemical properties. This simple, empirically driven approach, aims at improving performance by harnessing the “synergies” between the blend components. While this strategy has long been used in primary batteries, the extension of the approach for the Li-ion technology dates back to 2001 (NEI corporation) [10], the main aim being to mitigate capacity fading in LMO through mixing with LiNi0.8Co0.2O2. This combination helps create a locally alkaline pH environment, preventing manganese dissolution, which is otherwise promoted by acidic impurities in the electrolyte. Despite this strategy quickly attracted the interest of battery manufacturers, the fundamental study of redox mechanisms in these electrodes did not receive the attention of the scientific community. Even if a few meaningful studies were carried out by relevant groups in the field, mainly addressing the manganese dissolution issue [11,12], a review published in 2014 by researchers at Ford Motor Company mentions only ca. 15 studies on blends, highlighting the limited academic focus on this approach [13]. The motivation for using blends soon extended beyond cycle life aiming also at enhancing energy density, power performance and cost. For transport applications for instance, a common approach is to mix low cost LiMn2O4, known for its good power performance, with NMC or NCA, which exhibit higher capacity despite slower kinetics, limited thermal stability and higher cost. To this day, a significant number of commercial EVs contained Li-ion cells with different geometries all using graphite at the negative electrode and binary or even ternary blends at the positive electrode. A representative set of examples is shown in Table 1 . The widespread adoption of this approach can be assessed from studies on aging of cells used for automotive applications, which have identified numerous models employing blended positive electrodes [14–16].

While blending in positive electrodes used in EV batteries is primarily aimed at optimizing performance and mitigating degradation mechanisms, its role in silicon-based negative electrodes serves a different purpose. Due to the extreme volume expansion of silicon during lithiation (over three times its original size) blending with graphite helps buffer these mechanical stresses, improving cycle life and structural integrity [24,25]. This approach enables the partial incorporation of silicon (typically 8–10 % by weight) while leveraging graphite's mechanical stability and lower polarization to maintain electrode durability [26,27]. Despite its high theoretical capacity (around 3500 mAh/g, associated to the formation of Li15Si4, the most lithiated phase achievable by electrochemical means) [28]. The development of electrodes with large amounts of silicon remains constrained due to the important volume changes at each charge/discharge cycle of the battery, which induce significant capacity fading [29]. Aside the thousands of papers devoted to improving performance by playing on materials processing or formulation, relatively fewer studies have addressed the mechanistic aspects related to unraveling graphite and silicon contributions as a function of state of charge and operation conditions [26,30,31].

The early lack of fundamental studies can be attributed to the focus on pure compounds, which is better suited to elucidate structure-property correlations. Demonstrating and analyzing the interaction between components can be challenging and had to be inferred from indirect measurements including both electrochemical tests and post-mortem studies [12,32,33]. However, the seminal work by Heubner et al. [34] significantly advanced the understanding of blended electrodes, providing both a foundational framework and also new methodologies for future research.

Blended electrodes consist of two (or more) active materials in direct contact, each with different redox potentials and voltage curves, which may interact electrochemically to achieve equilibrium. Clearly distinguishing the contribution of each component to the electrode capacity is only feasible if their redox potentials differ significantly (e.g. on the order of hundreds of mV) and if the current flowing through the electrode is sufficiently low in order to avoid polarization effects.

However, even under these conditions, differences in their reaction kinetics can complicate the analysis. If both components provide capacity at a certain state of charge (whether due to thermodynamic or kinetic factors) their redox processes may overlap, making it difficult to deconvolute their individual contributions. Moreover, exchange of lithium between the blend components takes place within the electrode, as these are in contact. Indeed, at high operation rates, internal lithium redistribution between components to reach equilibrium can occur during relaxation periods [35], a phenomenon termed “buffer effect” [36].

The redox activity of each component (including the buffer effect) can in fact be directly assessed using a three electrode setup developed by Heubner et al. [37] adapted from galvanic corrosion studies. This system includes two short-circuited working electrodes, each one containing one of the blend components, along with a counter electrode. The current/potential is monitored with a potentiostat between the counter and the working electrodes, while the “short-circuiting” current between the two working electrodes is measured. This setup enables precise evaluation of the individual contributions to the total cell current during operation at different rates and subsequent relaxation, and has been instrumental for elucidating the behavior of different types of blends. Additionally, modified versions of this setup have been employed to further refine the understanding of blended electrodes potentially with more accuracy by replacing the multimeter-based ammeter initially used with a potentiostat [38,39], as seen schematically in Fig. 1 below.

The advantage of the configuration shown in Fig. 1 right is that it does not introduce a resistance between the two electrodes which better reflects the true blended system. For this case though, care must be taken for the potentiostat channels used to be electrically isolated to avoid stray currents, or, if they exist, make sure they are negligible.

On the mechanical design of the cell, the Swagelok T-shaped cell, widely used in battery research, can be utilized, with a counter electrode that bears holes to provide a direct lithium-conductive path for the working electrodes to exchange lithium ions. A different design is the one used to study Silicon/Graphite based negative electrodes by Heubner et al. [27] using a linear configuration (similar to normal two electrode Swagelok cell), with one of the plungers modified to accommodate more than one individual working electrodes.

Another approach is the one utilized by Knorr et al. [40] also on silicon-graphite negative electrodes. Interestingly, in this work two separate coin cells with the pure materials were used. The cells were connected in parallel, each having a 1 Ohm shunt resistor in series. By measuring the voltage drop on the resistors, one can calculate the current of each cell. This is arguably the easiest to make setup and can yield reliable results for the individual currents. At the same time though, for studies of charge exchange between the active materials, a lithium ion has to cross two extra lithium-electrolyte interfaces which could heavily underestimate its kinetics. Last, in this system the two active materials are chemically isolated, so any chemical interaction (e.g. involving the electrolyte) is ignored. A schematic representation of the three configurations is shown in Fig. 2 .

This setup enables the measurement of the effective rate on each blend component (which can be contrasted to the cell nominal rate). It has been shown that the effective rate each component experiences can exhibit notable fluctuations and, depending on the composition and the state of charge (SoC), the current could match the cell total intensity or even drop to zero (see Fig. 3a). And each component current will define its C-rate. The highest rate fluctuations occur when the cell voltage aligns with the reaction potential of one component where this particular component's effective rate increases while the other one's decreases. The relative amount of each blend component also significantly impacts these values, which showcases that any rate-related effect on a material will be exacerbated in blended systems [39]. These are crucial factors to consider and when possible take advantage of, when designing blended electrodes for specific applications. In Fig. 3b, one can see the effect of blend formulation on the rate experienced by each component for the NMC-532/LMO system. There are two main points to be taken out of these experiments. Each material's C-rate fluctuates heavily as a function of state of charge, as dictated firstly by its characteristic curve (plateaus or sloping regions) and secondly by each material's capacity contribution in the blend. The one with the lower capacity is expected to experience larger fluctuations than the other one.

Beyond rate-dependent effects, the decoupled blend methodology can also provide insights into lithium exchange between the two active materials through the buffer effect. The direction and magnitude of lithium redistribution depends on both the voltage profile of the materials and the composition of the blend [38]. When the two materials exhibit substantially different kinetic behaviors, the buffer effect is especially significant. Additionally, at high operation rates, lithium redistribution during relaxation can be more pronounced, influencing power performance and stability over prolonged cycling. In Fig. 4 the relaxation current between the two active materials is shown (red and green areas), as measured with the decoupled blend cell, for the 50:50 NMC/LFMP system. This measurement can predict the direction and magnitude variation of the lithium exchange between the materials when a blended electrode is subjected to intermittent high current. Knowing this could help explain and predict their performance as part of real-life application. It's worth noting that kinetics in every system is much more difficult to forecast as any change in secondary parameters such as particle size, electrode porosity, fabrication parameters could affect it and as such should be assessed in every individual case.

The decoupled setup can also be applied to silicon blends [27,41]. A striking difference with the positive electrode case is that the materials exhibit very different electrochemical capacities, as silicon has roughly ten times the specific capacity of graphite. Indeed, a 30 % mass of silicon leads the graphite to experience effective rates up to 4× the cell nominal one while the effective rate for silicon increased only from 0.05C to 0.065C. If we assume that, for certain SoC, the entire current goes to one single component, then the effective rate of this component, here understood as the “electrochemical stress” experienced, will highly depend on its capacity rather than its mass. This means that a low mass fraction of a component with a low specific capacity will experience a very significant increase in its effective rate.

The decoupled blend cell setup can also be used to assess thermo-electrochemical aspects. Temperature-driven lithium redistribution can occur due to differences in entropy coefficients between the blend components. Variations in temperature modify the Gibbs free energy of each material differently (initially identical at equilibrium), leading to spontaneous lithium exchange between them. The magnitude of this transfer is directly linked to the entropy coefficient of each compound, and can be significant as shown by Heubner et al. (Fig. 5 ) [35,42].

This effect could potentially influence cell performance not only during self-heating caused by battery operation but also during parking, where ambient temperature fluctuations drive lithium redistribution.

Last, it is worth noting that even though this setup provides valuable insights for the studied system, one has to bear in mind that the materials are physically separated and as such any effects stemming from the spatial proximity of them as part of blended electrodes could differ from the blend. These effects could be local chemical environments that could affect cycle life or microstructural effects (e.g. role of graphite volume in buffering the expansion/contraction or cracking of silicon particles in silicon:graphite blends). Studies using decoupled blend cells and real blended electrodes are complementary and enable to obtain a global picture of the underlying phenomena taking place.

Electrochemical characterization of blended electrodes can be approached using the same techniques typically applied to conventional electrodes, yet due to the added complexity, analysis could differ. Generally, electrochemical characterization techniques fall under three categories according to how the input signal is applied: continuous, intermittent or “pulse-like”, and impedance spectroscopy (frequency-based).

Continuous techniques include galvanostatic cycling, where a constant current is applied to the electrode and its degree of lithiation varies at a constant rate while the potential is recorded. In contrast, cyclic voltammetry involves sweeping the potential at a controlled rate while the resulting current is recorded. Both techniques can be used to assess the effect of blending on the reaction potentials of an electrode, as well as on the reaction kinetics during cycling.

It has been shown that blending does not affect the thermodynamic properties of the components [43]. Therefore, the effects observed are expected to be kinetic, stemming from the rate of (de)lithiation of each material, which will not necessarily match the rate applied to the electrode. Although the behavior of the blended electrode will include the characteristics of its components, those characteristics are expected to be rate-dependent. This highlights the importance of high rate testing of such systems, as this is where synergistic phenomena tend to appear. This is not always trivial, as differences in electrode processing (loading, calendering, etc.) and measurement parameters (temperature, cell design, etc.) also impact performance.

Intermittent techniques apply a time-dependent current (or potential) profile to the cell. These techniques include potentiostatic intermittent titration technique (PITT) and galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT); both have been extensively used in pure systems for many years and as such have also been applied to blended electrodes [44,45]. These techniques usually aim to quantify the mobility of lithium ions within the electrode. GITT is widely used, allowing (de)lithiation to take place using multiple current pulses, each followed by a relaxation step during which the open-circuit potential is recorded. The data obtained can be analyzed to extract apparent diffusion coefficients [46] or interpreted in terms of impedance characteristics [47]. This provides a means of quantifying the mobility of lithium as a function of voltage, which will likely vary with the degree of lithiation of both materials and electrode-related parameters. Great care must be taken before extracting absolute values for chemical diffusivities from [48] GITT in such systems since their complexity might yield untrustworthy results. As mentioned above, lithium redistribution can occur between the different blend components after a current pulse; this phenomenon might therefore be studied with GITT and comparisons made with pure components. On another hand, during PITT, small potential steps are applied to the cell and the current is monitored, enabling thus to assess the thermodynamics of the system, which may be particularly useful if blend components are active in a similar potential range. The current measured can then be integrated to give the incremental capacity (IC) gained with each potential step. Plotting this against voltage produces a plot with sharp peaks corresponding to the voltage at which a redox reaction occurs, allowing precise determination of the potential against lithium at which a transition happens. IC plots taken under different conditions (e.g. after different amounts of cycles or with different blend compositions) can be compared, and differences in peak position or intensity can be used to identify the effects of degradation due to aging, changes in local chemical environment etc. [48–52].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a widely used technique in battery research and has been applied to blended systems. By measuring the response of the system to a sinusoidal potential perturbation signal one can model the interfaces of the system, understand how ion movement takes place, and calculate internal resistances. EIS has been extensively applied to studies of cells with pure electrodes [51,53], allowing the lithium-ion conductivity of electrode materials to be calculated, and to blend electrodes, where the ionic and electronic contributions of the two blend components were analyzed [54,55].

Blending creates new interfaces between active materials, yet due to the complexity of the system, a full equivalent circuit could be difficult to achieve. That been said, EIS is a valuable and versatile tool for the analysis of such systems but full analysis of the spectra can take great effort.

In addition to electrochemical experiments, operando measurements, where a certain technique is applied during battery operation are also a useful tool to study blended electrodes. Beyond the intrinsic advantage of characterizing the materials during real operating conditions (thus avoiding the risk of materials evolving or being contaminated during cell disassembly and sample preparation procedures) [54], they also enable the indirect monitoring of the evolution of individual blend components, which can be correlated to specific operation conditions. X-ray diffraction (XRD) is most useful to that respect, as it enables to follow structural changes associated to redox operation (phase evolution and unit cell volume, which in turn can be directly correlated to degree of lithiation), with X-ray absorption (XAS) being an ideal complement, probing changes in the transition metal oxidation states. Synchrotron X-ray sources allow for fast data acquisition probing either a single cell or several cells sequentially [56] and coupling of different techniques in a multi-modal approach is currently the focus of intense activities with the objective of accelerating progress in battery research [57].

Solid-state NMR is a versatile tool for investigating the stability, phase transformations, and phase interactions within blend electrodes. As a quantitative technique sensitive to the local chemical environments, it provides insights into lithium distribution, revealing the fraction of Li ions in each phase and their degree of lithiation. In addition, it is a unique tool for probing dynamic processes, such as lithium transport and phase interactions, which are critical in blend electrodes. An example of this capability is the recent work of Kasnatscheew et al. [58] who employed solid-state NMR to demonstrate the transfer of lithium from graphite to silicon and compared its kinetics for various Si morphologies. Following this work, future studies could explore the impact of temperature, state of charge, and cycling history on transfer-lithiation. An even more direct way to probe these dynamic processes is through operando solid-state NMR, which enables real-time observation of lithiation mechanisms and phase interactions during battery operation. These techniques are particularly suitable for Si/graphite blends, where the Li signals are rather well-resolved [59]. However, for positive electrode blends like NMC/LNMO which are often paramagnetic and require fast magic angle spinning (MAS) for resolving the broad and overlapped signals, operando NMR presents more challenges. Ex situ NMR measurements, in contrast, offer higher resolution thanks to MAS and are more suitable for detailed analysis of the reaction mechanisms and phase transformations [60,61]. Additionally, performing these measurements at various temperatures could allow to identify Li environments that undergo a chemical exchange at a rate faster than the NMR timescale, thus providing further structural and dynamic insights [59].

Another promising approach for understanding phase interactions in blend electrodes could be the use of 7Li/6Li isotope exchange experiments, which take advantage of the significantly different natural abundances of Li isotopes. By enriching one phase with 6Li and tracking its redistribution over time [62] or upon cell cycling [63], this technique provides insights into lithium diffusion pathways and cross-talk between active materials. Nevertheless, despite its merits and capabilities for providing structural and dynamic information, solid-state NMR represents an underutilized tool for investigating the phase interactions in blend electrodes.

While the utility of a decoupled electrode setup for quantifying the electrochemical activity of individual blend components has been discussed above, one potential limitation of this setup is that it may not capture short-range local interactions, where variations in local overpotentials between adjacent blend components might tip the balance of their open-circuit potentials (OCPs) and give rise to additional lithium cross-talks [64]. Modeling offers a powerful alternative, as it provides direct access to the internal variables of each blend component and facilitates the investigation of effects that are otherwise challenging to measure. Moreover, predictive models can accelerate development and optimization of battery lifespan by enabling rational electrode and cell designs that account for these interactions, and they can serve as real-time SoX estimators in Battery Management Systems (BMSs).

A straightforward method for modeling battery behavior is to employ empirical models. Particularly, Equivalent Circuit Models (ECMs) have proven useful for representing the electrochemical response of blended electrodes under varying loads. Parallel ECMs (PECMs) are especially suited for such systems, since the parallel connection of each blend component to a voltage source describing its distinct open-circuit potential allows to describe their individual load shares [65,66]. These models, primarily used for real-time state estimators, offer a simple approach to capture effects like capacity enhancement or loss due to load imbalances where low resistance components effectively accommodate larger-than-nominal currents, allowing for the higher resistance component to receive more moderate currents and, in turn, have a capacity gain. However, while PECMs can qualitatively reflect inter-material dynamics, they lack the resolution needed for predictive electrode design or accurate monitoring of battery states at the electrode level and often depend on fitted parameters rather than physical descriptors. In contrast, physics-based models offer a more mechanistic understanding by simulating electrochemical and transport phenomena at the particle and electrode scale. These models allow for a detailed investigation of the internal dynamics of blended electrodes and have been successful in reproducing blended electrode behavior for different active material blending ratios [67,68]. These early models have demonstrated good agreement with experimental data, but their primary focus has often been on fitting observable behavior rather than uncovering the fundamental mechanisms driving interactions between the active material components in electrode blends. Recent advances have incorporated the porous electrode structure and the particle-size distribution [44,69], unraveling the current balancing between active materials (the “buffer effect”) during current pulses and rest periods. Additionally, newer efforts have focused on capturing active material topology by considering the particle-to-particle connectivity and size distributions of primary particles within agglomerates of various morphologies [70]. This general framework opens up new possibilities for application in multi-material blends, enabling precise and computationally efficient models to capture the interaction between active materials.

These above described models provide a homogenized description of the battery electrode, using volume averaged physical laws. However, in recent years, mesoscale models have attracted significant attention as it has become clear that battery performance is strongly influenced by microstructural heterogeneities, especially under high current loads, with these effects being even more pronounced in blended electrodes. These high-fidelity models enable the identification of large local reaction currents, overpotentials, and lithium fluxes that affect heat generation [71,72], which homogenized models fail to capture fully. Consequently, these localized phenomena play an important role in degradation mechanisms such as SEI layer growth, lithium plating [73], electrolyte degradation [71], and active material cracking [74]. In electrode blends, these effects may be further magnified by the unequal current distribution among the blend components. However, fully resolved mesoscale models for blended electrodes are scarce, with most research focusing on negative electrodes made of silicon and graphite, where the primary focus has been put on mechanical effects [75] and lithium plating [76]. Interestingly, Liu et al. showed that at low discharge current rates, graphite can momentarily carry more than the nominal current load while silicon is delithiating. Although this phenomenon appears to diminish at higher rates, a closer inspection of the microstructure reveals that even under such conditions, a portion of the active silicon surface exhibits delithiation current densities [75], providing further evidence of lithium cross-talk between blend components.

Finally, to the best of our knowledge, no fully resolved mesoscale models exist for positive electrode blends, suggesting a promising opportunity for the development of predictive models that accurately capture the abovementioned complex interactions.

In summary, blending could a priori affect a number of different aspects and the following need to be considered when designing them:
i
By applying simple geometric principles, combining two materials with different particle sizes (even of the same chemical composition) can directly impact packing density, thereby increasing the energy density of the electrode.
ii
Chemically, in the case of NMC-LMO blends, it has been proposed that NMC can act as a proton scavenger altering the pH around LMO as such protecting it from HF attack formed by the hydrolysis of LiPF6 and, as such, limit the acid-mediated manganese dissolution of LMO, improving its cycle life.
iii
Electrochemically, as the potential vs. capacity profile of the blend will depend on that of the components, so that some aspects can be tailored by design to help with state of charge (SoC) estimation, which is relevant for the battery management system (BMS).
iv
In terms of kinetics, the “buffer effect” represents a synergistic interaction between the two materials the extent of which is related to the difference in kinetics between them for a given SoC.
v
The thermal behavior will also be impacted, even when batteries are at rest, due to the difference in entropy coefficients between components. Thus, temperature changes induce exchanges of lithium which may even negatively affect cycle life if frequent and of a significant magnitude.

Designing effective blended electrodes requires thus a careful balance of multiple factors, as the interplay between component properties can significantly impact performance. A key design consideration is voltage curve overlap of the two materials under different C-rates (thermodynamics), as it affects how the two materials contribute to the total electrode capacity at each SoC. The effective current load on each blend component varies also as a function of blending ratio, with the minority component experiencing much higher electrochemical stress. This means that in rational blend design, minority components should have fast reaction kinetics to ensure a net performance gain rather than causing bottlenecks. Furthermore, these effective current peaks strongly influence redox and phase change reactions, potentially impacting long-term cycling performance [39]. In turn, the blending fraction plays a crucial role in the resulting voltage profile and therefore will impact directly the aspects above. If a given material provides the entire cell current at a certain voltage, its effective operating rate will be dictated by its fraction within the blend. For example, in a hypothetical blend where materials A and B have equal theoretical capacities, if A constitutes only 20 % of the active mass, but takes the entire current at a certain voltage, its effective rate will be 5 times higher than the nominal cell rate. In contrast, if B provides all the current, its effective rate will be only 1.25 times higher. As a result, small fractions of a material like LFP with a plateau in the voltage profile will experience exceedingly high rates when the cell voltage aligns with that plateau. Additionally, when polarization is asymmetric between charge and discharge, the current distribution between components can vary significantly between oxidation and reduction, making it difficult to determine the optimal blend formulation.

One also interesting, but hardly studied mechanism of blending is that of non-mixed (or segregated) blended electrode, meaning the two components are coated one on top of the other or generally in discreet clusters rather than mixed, materials coated on top of the other make the so-called dual-layer electrodes (DL's). It has been reported for the NMC-LFP system [77] that placing the LFP layer on top in a ratio of 30:70 with NMC can kinetically outperform both pure electrodes as well as the homogeneously blended one of the same formulation. Other older studies have also shown improvements by using such design, when implemented properly [78–80]. This type of electrode design seems promising though remains understudied.

The aspects discussed above have been mainly considered for positive electrode materials blends. In contrast, even if blending graphite and silicon is common for the negative electrode, they have seldom been considered under the same optics. As mentioned above, when blending materials with a significant difference in relative capacities, the electrochemical stress experienced by the component with the lower capacity will be exacerbated.

Finally, kinetic disparities between components leading to internal lithium redistribution during operation, (buffer effect) should be considered. Since overpotential varies with SoC, blending can be used as a strategy to relieve or stress operation at specific voltages. The extent of the buffer effect depends on the kinetics of each component, the applied current rates, and the blending ratio. Aspects such as particle size, porosity, and tortuosity can also be leveraged as additional tuning parameters.

Even if the studies and aspects discussed above represent a leap forward in the development of blended electrodes to leave empiricism in favor of rational design, many open challenges remain. In practical applications, and especially the ones used in electric vehicles, cells are rarely operated at constant current. High intensity pulses and regenerative braking impose complex polarization dynamics, which impact each of the blend's components differently, and which will depend on the specific current and effective rate, and fluctuate also as a function of state of charge. Studying lithium dynamics in both positive and negative blended electrodes in such real case scenarios could help in their design to improve both performance and lifetime in next generation lithium-ion batteries. Coupling this effort with operando techniques such as XRD, XAS, and NMR (especially when combined in multimodal approaches) can offer invaluable insight into phase-specific behaviors, redox kinetics, and lithium distribution at relevant timescales and spatial resolution.

In parallel with experimental advances, predictive modeling of blended electrodes remains underdeveloped. Most existing models are tailored to specific chemistries or rely heavily on empirical parameter tuning, limiting their applicability. Versatile, multi-scale models that can bridge particle-, electrode-, and cell-level phenomena are needed to replicate experimental data, but also to predictively guide blend formulation.

Finally, establishing a robust feedback loop between simulation and experiment will be essential to fully unlock the potential of complex blends, not just for Li-ion, but also for emerging chemistries such as Na-ion.
